text: The mechanism by which copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions cleave carbon-halogen bonds has remained poorly understood, largely due to the diverse redox pathways available to copper and the high reactivity of the resulting high-valent intermediates. Here, we report the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitileres to both ionic and neutral copper(I) complexes, resulting in the formation of previously elusive alkyl copper(III) products. These complexes were fully characterized, and their unusual stability is attributed to the presence of a strong Cu–CF₃ bond combined with a substantial energy barrier that prevents C(CF₃)–C(CH₂CN) reductive elimination. Our mechanistic investigations, supported by kinetic studies and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, reveal that oxidative addition follows two distinct trajectories depending on the nature of the precursor: the ionic copper(I) complex reacts primarily through an Sₙ2-type substitution, whereas the neutral complex proceeds via halogen-atom transfer (XAT). Notably, we observed significant ligand acceleration in the neutral system, a phenomenon that aligns with the reactivity patterns seen in the copper-catalyzed coupling of alkyl electrophiles with amines, azoles, and alkynes. These findings provide a rare, detailed look at the fundamental steps of copper-mediated bond formation.
